This paper fundamentally does not fit the SPEED framework. It is a study of AI-designed economic policies (tax schedules learned via reinforcement learning) compared to baseline tax policies, not a study of human worker productivity with versus without AI assistance. The 'AI' in 'AI Economist' refers to the mechanism that designed the tax policy, not a tool that assists human workers in performing tasks. The comparisons are between policy mechanisms (AI Economist vs. free market vs. US federal vs. Saez formula), not between human workers performing tasks with vs. without AI assistance. In the AI experiments, AI agents play an economic simulation game under different tax policies. In the human experiments, human participants play the same game under different tax policies. In both cases, the outcome (productivity = coins produced) is a measure of the tax policy's effect on economic outcomes, not a measure of human worker productivity with/without AI assistance. There is no human-only baseline arm in the sense required for SPEED. The 'free market' arm is a no-tax policy baseline, not a human-only work condition. The paper reports means for productivity under each tax policy (Figure 8: AI experiments - Free Market 3285, US Federal 2656, Saez 2907, AI Economist 2907; Figure 17: human experiments - Free Market 2605, US Federal 1658, Saez 1658, AI Economist 2339), but these are outcomes of the tax policies themselves, not measures of human worker productivity with/without AI assistance. Sample sizes are reported as number of episodes or participants across all conditions (Section 5.2: 'first batch consisted of 57 episodes with 58 participants', 'second batch consisted of 68 episodes with 57 participants'), not per-arm in a way that allows extraction for a human vs. human+AI comparison. The paper does report that the AI Economist achieves 16% improvement in equality-productivity trade-off over Saez (page 20: 'The AI Economist achieves a 16% gain improvement over the next best model, which is the Saez model'), but this is a composite metric (equality × productivity) comparing policy outcomes, not a human productivity comparison. No standard deviations, standard errors, t-statistics, or regression coefficients comparing human-only vs. human+AI productivity are reported in a way that fits the SPEED framework. The paper does report statistical tests for the human experiments (Section 5.2: 'two-sided t-test for the alternate hypothesis Ya;vw ≠ 0, with p = 0.05'), but these compare tax policy regimes, not human+AI vs. human-only productivity. After exhaustive re-reading, I confirm that this paper does not contain the data needed to compute a SPEED effect size. | arm pair unresolvable

This is a paired-design comparison where each of 27 bankers produced human-AI collaborative advice for 10 loans (randomly selected from 24), and the AI produced advice for all 24 loans. For each banker, accuracy on their 10 loans was compared to AI accuracy on the same 10 loans. The mean difference in accuracy (human-AI minus AI-only) is -0.03 across 27 bankers, tested with a one-sample t-test (p=0.23). The design is within-subject (paired) because each banker's accuracy is compared to AI accuracy on the same set of loans. However, the SD of the 27 difference scores is not reported, nor is the t-statistic. Only the mean difference (-0.03), sample size (27), and p-value (0.23) are provided. Python may attempt to back-calculate the t-statistic from p and df=26, then derive the SD, but this introduces uncertainty. The outcome is investment recommendation accuracy (proportion of correct recommendations), where larger is better. The comparison is AI-only (baseline) vs. Human-AI (treatment). Note: the paper's primary focus is on consumer response (Stage 2), but this production-side accuracy comparison (Stage 1) is the only quality outcome with reported statistics comparing human-AI to AI-only advice. | insufficient numbers for d

Table A7 Panel B reports intent-to-treat effects on Critical CVEs. For the copilot_ineligible arm (baseline), mean = 7.801 (SE = 0.248), n = 76,662. The treatment coefficient is -1.060*** (SE = 0.305), p < 0.001. The AI arm mean can be inferred as 7.801 - 1.060 = 6.741. However, the table does not report the SD for the AI arm directly; only the baseline SD is available. The paper states '33.9% lower' in the text, which is consistent with the coefficient. Without the AI arm SD or a test statistic that allows direct computation, we can only provide the baseline mean, baseline SD (converted from SE using n), the treatment effect, and the p-value. The baseline SD = SE * sqrt(n) = 0.248 * sqrt(76662) ≈ 68.6. This is implausibly large for a count outcome, suggesting the SE may be robust/clustered. Given the large sample size and the reported coefficient with SE, Python may be able to approximate the effect size using the regression coefficient and baseline SD, though this is not ideal. The paper states: 'We also find that the treated group's success rate is 7 percentage points higher than the control group, but the estimate is not statistically significant, with a 95% confidence interval of [-0.11, 0.25].' Only the raw difference (7 percentage points) and CI are provided. No means, SDs, SEs, test statistics (t, F), or standardized effect sizes are reported for this outcome. The p-value is described only as 'not statistically significant' with no exact value. The CI bounds [-0.11, 0.25] appear to be for the proportion difference itself, not a standardized effect. Without at least one of: (a) means+SDs, (b) means+SEs, (c) t-statistic+df, (d) F-statistic, (e) reported d/g, or (f) exact p-value with sufficient detail, Hedges' g cannot be computed. The primary outcome in this study is task completion time (55.8% reduction, p=0.0017), not task success rate, which is reported as a secondary finding with minimal statistical detail. | arm pair unresolvable

This is a descriptive survey study of 156 employees in technological firms in Delta State, Nigeria, with 108 questionnaires returned (69% response rate). The study tests three hypotheses using one-sample t-tests. For Hypothesis 1 (AI has positive and significant effect on employee productivity), the calculated t-value is 9.110 with df=4, table t-value=1.812, leading to rejection of null hypothesis. The study design appears to be cross-sectional comparing employees in organizations with AI vs without AI, though the exact arm structure is not entirely clear from the methodology section. The sample size of 108 appears to represent the total respondents, and the statistical tests suggest comparison conditions, but raw means and SDs for productivity outcomes are not reported in the results tables. The study reports Likert-scale survey responses but does not provide the aggregated mean productivity scores that would allow direct effect size calculation. The t-statistic from Hypothesis 1 is the strongest quantitative evidence available for the AI effect on productivity. | no usable numbers

This paper is primarily a survey-based study examining cognitive processes, motivation, and perceptions rather than measuring objective speed or quality outcomes. The study reports percentages of students who exhibit certain behaviors or hold certain beliefs (e.g., 38% try independently first, 70% feel motivated by AI solutions, 68% believe AI helps them progress faster). However, these are self-reported perceptions, not measured productivity or speed outcomes. The sample sizes are derived from the total N=131 respondents, with 20% (approximately 26) not using AI tools and the remainder (approximately 105) using AI tools. The paper does not report means, standard deviations, or effect sizes for any objective speed or quality measure. The '68% believe AI helps them progress faster' is a perception, not a measured speed outcome. Without actual performance data (time to complete tasks, number of tasks completed, etc.), a speed effect size cannot be computed from this paper. | no usable numbers

The paper reports a negative carryover effect where volunteers introduced to AI reduce their monthly contributions by 21.3% on average (Table 1: coefficient -0.240, p<0.01). The dependent variable is ln(Tasks_it), which is log-transformed. The paper uses a difference-in-differences design with volunteer and month fixed effects. The matched sample has 360 treated and 360 control volunteers. However, the paper does not report raw means, SDs, or other statistics needed to compute Hedges' g directly. The reported coefficient is from a regression model with fixed effects on a log-transformed outcome, which cannot be directly converted to Hedges' g without additional information. The percentage reduction (21.3%) is calculated as e^(-0.240) - 1. While the paper provides extensive robustness checks and heterogeneity analysis (Tables EC.12-EC.23), none report the raw statistics needed for effect size calculation. The CIC analysis (Table 3) shows quantile treatment effects but these are also not in a form convertible to Hedges' g. | insufficient numbers for d

The paper reports regression coefficients rather than simple treatment vs. control means. At similarity=0 (fully human-revised AI titles), the treatment effect would be 0.016, giving an implied mean of ~0.525 for treatment. This is a 'Matters Arising' commentary paper that re-analyzes Lee & Chung (2024) data. The paper does NOT measure speed/productivity/throughput as its primary outcome. Instead, it measures DIVERSITY of idea sets using metrics like 'percentage of unique ideas' and cosine similarity between ideas. The primary finding is that ChatGPT-aided idea sets have LOWER diversity (e.g., Experiment 1: web-search 0.96 vs ChatGPT 0.74 unique fraction; Experiment 2a: human 0.92, web-search 0.91, ChatGPT 0.70; Experiment 2b: human-only 1.00, ChatGPT-human 0.41, ChatGPT-only 0.06). These are NOT speed metrics. The paper explicitly states it is analyzing 'diversity of ideas' and 'originality at the set level' rather than individual creativity scores or completion time. The original Lee & Chung paper may have measured creativity scores, but this commentary focuses exclusively on diversity metrics. There are no means, SDs, or effect sizes reported for any speed/productivity outcome. Sample sizes are mentioned in passing (e.g., '20 out of 96 ChatGPT-aided ideas included the word sprinkler') but these refer to idea counts, not participant counts for a speed measure. The paper is fundamentally about a different outcome dimension (diversity) than what the SPEED extraction schema requires (productivity/throughput/time). | no usable numbers

This paper does not have a traditional RCT design with separate human-only and AI arms. Instead, it's an observational study of 2,047 GitHub Copilot users who completed a survey. The analysis correlates usage metrics (particularly acceptance rate = accepted_per_shown) with self-reported aggregate productivity scores. The key finding is a Pearson correlation coefficient ρ = 0.24 (P < 0.0001) between acceptance rate and aggregate productivity. This is NOT a mean difference or percent change in throughput, but rather a correlation coefficient. The paper states: 'Of all usage measurements, acceptance rate correlates best with aggregate productivity (ρ = 0.24, P < 0.0001).' The outcome 'contribution_speed' (characters per hour) is one of many metrics examined, but the primary finding is about the correlation with perceived productivity, not a direct comparison of speed with vs. without AI. The sample size n=2047 represents survey respondents matched to usage data. Without separate human-only and AI arms, or reported means/SDs for contribution_speed, we cannot compute a traditional log ratio of means. The regression_coefficient_value of 0.24 is the correlation coefficient, not a regression coefficient in the traditional sense for computing effect sizes. This study design does not cleanly map to the SPEED extraction schema. | regression coefficient on levels but baseline mean unknown

After careful re-reading, this paper is definitively an observational correlational study, not a controlled experiment or A/B test. All 2,631 survey respondents were users of GitHub Copilot during the technical preview. The study's core contribution is establishing that acceptance rate (the fraction of shown completions that developers accept) correlates with self-reported productivity (ρ = 0.24, P < 0.0001). The paper explicitly states its goal: 'we investigate whether usage measurements of developer interactions with GitHub Copilot can predict perceived productivity as reported by developers.' There is no baseline condition without AI assistance. The paper does reference a prior RCT (reference 16, Peng et al.) that found task completion time reductions, but the current study does not replicate that experimental design. Instead, it analyzes naturalistic variation in how developers engage with the tool. Therefore, no arm pair can be resolved for computing Hedges' g comparing human-only vs. human+AI performance. | arm pair unresolvable
